schools.summary: Matthew Henry reads Issachar through Jacob's blessing (Gen. 49:14) — an industrious tribe, numerous through its many wives and valiant, above 145,000 fit for war.
Gill matches the four sons to the older registers (Gen. 46:13; Num. 26:23), noting the small variation of two names.
Clarke lays out the whole chapter's design, tribe by tribe from Issachar to Asher.
Keil and Delitzsch parse the peculiar Hebrew — six heads of fathers'-houses of Tola, registered by births at 22,600 in David's day, resting probably on Joab's census.
Gill takes Tola's posterity to be reckoned here by name.
Clarke is cautious whether the figure came from Joab's ill-fated census, and marks that only three tribes are numbered under David for want of registers.

debates.question: From what census does the muster of the tribes derive?

sides.position: the results of Joab's numbering (2 Sam. 24; 1 Chron. 21)
uncertain; the source cannot be known
some separate computation, since Joab's census was never entered in the royal chronicles

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch trace five heads through Izrahiah with 36,000 men, and refute Bertheau's inference of a date later than David.
Gill takes the sons of Uzzi to include his posterity through Izrahiah.
Clarke meets the count of five where four are named by reckoning Izrahiah in with his four sons, noting the Syriac and Arabic read four.
Keil and Delitzsch give the 36,000 and the reason the text itself supplies — 'for they had many wives and sons.'
Gill glosses the bands of soldiers as trained companies who took the field on occasion.
Henry reads the fertile, industrious multitude as blessing counted in fighting men — let none complain of numbers, provided none be idle.
Keil and Delitzsch give the total — 87,000 valiant men reckoned by genealogy across all the families of Issachar.
Gill identifies the brethren with the houses of Puah, Jashub, and Shimron.
Henry adds the branches to above 145,000, praising a labouring people made mighty for war.
Clarke marks Issachar as one of only three tribes whose Davidic muster survives.

Clarke lays out the confusion of Benjamin's sons across Gen. 46:21, Num. 26:38, and the next chapter — grandsons called sons, and Ezra's tables often defective; Wesley: only three named, as most eminent or the rest extinct.
Ellicott reads Becher as 'firstborn' and Jediael ('friend of God') as a pious substitute for Ashbel/Eshbaal ('man of Baal'), like Eliada for Beeliada (1 Chron. 3:8).
Gill names three of Benjamin's ten sons and takes Jediael for Ashbel.

Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott take the five 'sons' of Bela to be clan-heads at David's census, the names differing from 1 Chron. 8:3 because that list belongs to another period — the Chronicler transcribing his sources without harmonising.
Gill thinks them grandsons of Bela, given the different names in the parallel.
Wesley likewise makes them heads of families, grandchildren rather than immediate sons.
Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that Anathoth and Alameth are also town-names, the Levitical towns of Benjamin (1 Chron. 6:60), the clans bearing the names of their seats.
Gill names the nine sons of Becher without further remark.

Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott give the census of Becher's house as 20,200, the chiefs reckoned as one with their clans; Ellicott adds the question whether the names are eponyms like Hellen, Ion, and Dorus.
Gill takes the number for the posterity of Becher's sons.

Ellicott finds in the sons of Jediael the marks of absorbed peoples — Bilhan a Horite name (1 Chron. 1:42), Jeush an Esau-name (1 Chron. 1:35), Chenaanah a Canaanite house, and a clan bearing the tribal name Benjamin.
Gill names the descendants of Jediael through Bilhan.

Keil and Delitzsch give Jediael's 17,200; Ellicott reckons Benjamin's whole muster at 54,434, set against the Mosaic 45,600 (Num. 26:41), the small increase owing to the near-extermination at Gibeah (Judg. 20:47).
Gill adds the branches to 59,430 and supposes Joab's account of it was never given in.

debates.question: What is the total muster of Benjamin?

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch call the verse all but unintelligible, taking Shuppim and Huppim for further clans of Ir (Iri son of Bela); Ellicott weighs and finally rejects the reading of 'Hushim the sons of Aher' as a veiled Dan, calling the whole an appendix to Benjamin.
Gill makes Shuppim and Huppim great-grandchildren of Benjamin, the Shupham and Hupham of the families.
Henry keeps alive the reading of Aher as 'another,' a hint of the idolatrous tribe of Dan (cf. Gen. 46:23), passed over here as at Rev. 7.

debates.question: Does 'Hushim the sons of Aher' conceal the omitted tribe of Dan?

sides.position: yes — a veiled reference to Dan, whose sons were Hushim (Gen. 46:23) and whose idolatry earned its silence
no — merely an appendix to the genealogy of Benjamin; Num. 26:42 calls Dan's son Shuham, and the Chronicler names Dan freely elsewhere

themes: the hardest verse in the chapter
Shuppim and Huppim as clans of Ir

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott match the four sons to Gen. 46:24 and Num. 26:48 (there Jahzeel and Shillem), note that only the sons and not the families are kept, and deny that 'sons of Bilhah' points to the missing Dan.
Gill glosses Shallum as the Shillem of the older lists.
Henry thinks the descendants unnamed because the tribe's genealogies had been lost.

Keil and Delitzsch own the verses very obscure, tracing Asriel to the sons of Gilead; Ellicott renders 'whom his Aramean concubine bare,' making Asriel a great-grandson of Manasseh through Machir (Num. 27:1; Josh. 17:3).
Gill reads 'she' of Manasseh's wife, distinguished from the concubine, though the Targum joins the clauses.
Wesley agrees: 'she' is the wife, the concubine set in opposition to her.

debates.question: Who bore Asriel — Manasseh's wife or his Aramean concubine?

sides.position: the wife, distinguished from the concubine
the Aramean concubine, mediately through descent from Machir

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott judge the received 'took to wife' impossible; Ellicott reads 'a wife of Huppim and of Shuppim' with a lacuna where the second wife's name and Zelophehad's descent have dropped, asserting Aramean and Benjamite blood in West Manasseh.
Gill takes it plainly: Machir married Maachah, sister of Huppim and Shuppim, into the tribe of Benjamin.
Wesley makes 'the second' Machir's second son or grandson, Zelophehad, who had daughters only.

debates.question: What does 'Machir took to wife' mean, and is the text sound?

sides.position: a plain marriage to Maachah, sister of the Benjamite Huppim and Shuppim
the Hebrew is corrupt; a second wife's name has fallen out, Zelophehad being a man

themes: Aramean and Benjamite blood in West Manasseh

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch carry the line through Peresh, Sheresh, Ulam, and Rakem, gathering them into 'the sons of Gilead'; Ellicott notes Peresh and Sheresh occur nowhere else and takes Ulam and Rakem for the elder Peresh's sons.
Gill records that Maachah, wife of Machir, bore Peresh and Sheresh.
Ellicott reads Bedan as 'ben-Dan,' here a clan not a person, and suspects the summary clause covers a series of names dropped from the text (cf. Num. 26:30).
Henry hopes to find in Bedan a deliverer of Israel (1 Sam. 12:11), perhaps Jair the Manassite (Judg. 10:3).
Wesley refers 'these were the sons of Gilead' back to Ashriel and Zelophehad, the relative pointing to the remoter antecedent.
Gill notes only the bare descent, the sons of Ulam being Bedan.

debates.question: Is Bedan here a clan or a person, and to whom does 'these' refer?

sides.position: a clan, not a person; the summary clause hides a dropped series of names
perhaps the deliverer Bedan of 1 Sam. 12:11, i.e. Jair the Manassite
the clause refers back to Ashriel and Zelophehad

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch make Hammoleketh Machir's sister and distinguish her son Abiezer from the elder Jeezer of Josh. 17:2 / Num. 26:30; Ellicott takes her for 'the queen,' half-sister and consort of Gilead.
Gill weighs the renderings — 'that reigned,' Kimchi's queen of part of Gilead, the Vulgate's 'a queen' — and ties Abiezer's line to Gideon.
Wesley reads 'his sister' of Gilead and carries 'and Shemida' over from the next verse.

themes: Hammoleketh, queen and sister of Gilead

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott insist the four sons of Shemida be kept distinct from their near-homonyms — this Shechem is not the Manassite Shechem (Num. 26:31; Josh. 17:2), nor Likhi the Helek, nor Aniam the Noam.
Gill derives the family of the Shemidaites (Num. 26:32) from this branch.

Henry makes Ephraim's line a meditation on providence crossing promise — the tribe wounded in its infancy in Egypt, yet the promise of a multitude of nations (Gen. 48:19) only magnified by the seeming failure.
Keil and Delitzsch trace Shuthelah's descendants through six generations and take Ezer and Elead as the family slain by the native men of Gath, rejecting the Avvite identification (Deut. 2:23).
Gill traces the plain five-generation descent of Ephraim.
Henry canvasses who struck first and inclines to the men of Gath raiding Ephraim's cattle, since the Israelites in Egypt were shepherds not soldiers; on the slain he follows Lightfoot.
Keil and Delitzsch read the native men of Gath as slaying Zabad, Shuthelah, Ezer, and Elead in a raid on the herds.
Clarke reports the Targum's tale of a mistimed exodus, reckoned wrongly from the covenant of the pieces (Gen. 15), that cost 200,000 Ephraimites their lives.
Gill takes Zabad for a second son of Ephraim himself.

debates.question: Who were the aggressors in the clash with Gath?

sides.position: the men of Gath came down to raid Ephraim's cattle
the Ephraimites made a premature descent to seize Canaan, and paid for their rashness

schools.summary: Henry pours out the pathos — the aged burying the young, and the brethren's friendly office of comfort, reminding Ephraim that the promise was sure though his house was a house of mourning (2 Sam. 23:5).
Gill fixes the fact against those who multiply Ephraims: the patriarch himself mourned, the deed done while Israel was yet in Egypt.
Keil and Delitzsch carry the mourning and the brethren's coming forward to the birth that would repair the breach.
Wesley reads the birth as the breach repaired in old age, God making glad according to the days of affliction (Ps. 90:15), yet the melancholy name Beriah keeping the memory of sorrow.
Ellicott explains the allusive play of Beriah ('gift') on bera'ah ('in evil'), like the wordplays at Gen. 5:29 and Gen. 11:9 — meant to point a moral, not to teach etymology.
Henry sees another Seth given for a slain Abel (Gen. 4:25).
Gill marks the tenderness of the moment, after grief had in part subsided.

Ellicott sets Sherah's 'building' (rebuilding, fortifying) of the two Beth-horons (Josh. 10:10) and Uzzen-sherah in its geography, comparing Achsah (Josh. 15:19); Keil and Delitzsch take her for an heiress enlarging her inheritance.
Gill makes her a descendant of Beriah who reached the times of Joshua.
Clarke glosses her name as 'remnant,' the Targum's escaped survivor of the slaughter.
Henry: a virtuous woman may honour a family as much as a mighty man.

themes: a woman's honour to the tribe

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch fix Elishama son of Ammihud in the Mosaic camp (Num. 1:10; 7:48) and note the odd form Non for Nun; Ellicott suspects a dropped 'his son' and reads Resheph as a Baal-title.
Gill runs the line from Rephah down through Elishama, prince of Ephraim, to Nun and Joshua.
Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch identify Elishama son of Ammihud as the tribal prince, the emir of Ephraim in the time of Moses, who bore the tribe's standard on the march (Num. 7:47; 10:22).
Gill likewise calls Elishama prince of the tribe of Ephraim in the wilderness.

Keil and Delitzsch bring the descent to rest in Joshua; Ellicott argues the seven or eight generations here against only two from Levi to Moses (1 Chron. 6:1) prove these genealogies are not to be read in a slavishly literal spirit.
Gill ends the line in Nun, whose son was Jehoshua, the successor of Moses.
Keil and Delitzsch map the four sides of Ephraim's domain and, from Bethel being counted to Ephraim rather than Benjamin (Josh. 16:2; 18:22), date the register to the days of the northern kingdom.
Gill takes the towns for the possessions of Ephraim once come into Canaan.
Keil and Delitzsch name the four border-cities Manasseh held within Issachar and Asher (Josh. 17:11), the northern bound of the dwellings of the sons of Joseph.
Gill sets the Ephraimites dwelling by the borders of Manasseh.
Keil and Delitzsch match Asher's four sons and their sister to Gen. 46:17, noting Ishuai is absent from the families in Num. 26:44.
Gill reckons Asher's sons and grandsons as in the law, with Malchiel added.
Henry praises a tribe few in number but excellent in its choice princes.

Keil and Delitzsch set Heber and Malchiel as heads of two families (Gen. 46:17; Num. 26:45) and leave the unique Birzavith undecided — a woman's name (Gesenius) or a place, 'well of the olive-tree' (Bertheau).
Gill marks Malchiel as Asher's second grandson, added beyond the older lists.
Henry keeps before the reader the worth of the small tribe's choice princes.
Keil and Delitzsch set out Heber's sons Japhlet, Shomer, and Hotham with Shua their sister, and suspect Hotham and the later Helem to be one man under two spellings.
Gill marks Heber as grandson of Asher, son of Beriah.
Clarke notes that women enter the Jewish genealogies only for some especial reason, as Shua does here.
Keil and Delitzsch set down Japhlet's three sons — Pasach, Bimhal, Ashvath — closing the branch with 'these are the children of Japhlet.'
Gill notes plainly that these are read of nowhere else.

themes: the least clan a full entry

schools.summary: Ellicott identifies Shamer with Shomer, prefers the margin 'Hubbah,' and reads Aram as a clan of half-Aramean blood; Keil and Delitzsch enumerate the sons Ahi, Rohgah, Jehubbah, and Aram.
Gill names Shamer, or Shomer, the brother of Japhlet.
Keil and Delitzsch take Helem for Heber's third son, elsewhere Hotham, one name a scribal error; Ellicott allows that Helem may instead be a distinct chief house, the 'brother' being tribal.
Gill, following Hillerus, identifies Helem with Hotham.

debates.question: Is Helem the same man as Hotham (7:32)?

sides.position: yes — one of the two names is a transcription error
perhaps not — Helem may be another chief house, the brotherhood tribal

themes: a scribe's slip or a tribal brother

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch count Zophah's house at eleven names; Ellicott marks Harnepher as pure Egyptian ('Har nefer,' the beauteous Horus), one of several traces of Egypt in these registers (cf. 1 Chron. 4:18).
Gill names Zophah the eldest of Helem's sons.

Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott gather Bezer, Hod, Shamma, Shilshah, Ithran, and Beera into the eleven sons of Zophah, the largest and most purely documentary branch of Asher, adding no story to the bare names.

Ellicott identifies Jether with Ithran, linking the verse to Zophah's line, and notes Jephunneh as the name of Caleb's father (Num. 13:6); Keil and Delitzsch give the three sons Jephunneh, Pispah, and Ara.
Gill takes Jether for the same as Ithran.
Ellicott reads Ulla as the Ara of the previous verse restored, gaining an unbroken concatenation from v.30 to v.39; Keil and Delitzsch give the sons Arah, Haniel, and Rezia.
Gill guesses Ulla the son of Ara, or another son of Jether.

Ellicott sums the picked chiefs and valiant warriors at 26,000 fit for war, set against the Mosaic 41,500 (Num. 1:41) and 53,000 (Num. 26:47), a decline in keeping with Deborah's reproach that Asher stayed by his creeks (Judg. 5:17).
Gill takes the summary at its plain sense: all heads of their fathers' houses, principal men.
Clarke adds the tradition that Asher's daughters were beautiful and matched with kings or priests.
Henry turns the small number to praise: choice and mighty princes, a few serviceable men.
